coredata.dc:title: Porous silica nanocarriers with gold/carbon quantum dots for photo-chemotherapy and cellular imaging
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Drug Delivery Science and Technology
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Yunyun
Zhao, Yanbao
Zou, Xueyan
Sun, Lei
coredata.dc:description: The integration of multiple treatment methods for improving therapy efficiency has been widely concerned. Herein, porous silica (pSiO2) carriers with center-radial channels were designed and synthesized, then the photosensitizer carbon dots (CDs) and antitumor drug doxorubicin (Dox) were adsorbed into the channels and Au nanoparticles (NPs) were also loaded in the pores of the pSiO2 carriers. Biocompatible hyaluronic acid/alginate/chitosan (HCA) multilayers were coated onto the pSiO2 carriers to seal the loaded-drug pores. The CDs are capable of generating singlet oxygen and multicolor imaging, and Au NPs have good photothermal effect. The obtained CDs/Dox-pSiO2-Au/HCA not only has enhanced synergistic chemotherapy, photothermal therapy (PTT) and photodynamic therapy (PDT), but also exhibits pH/redox/enzyme-triggered drug release.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Porous silica carriers
CDs
Au NPs
Multilayer
Responsive release
Drug delivery

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CDs and Au NPs
In vitro cytotoxicity against HCT-116 cells
Cell multicolor imaging and intracellular localization
Location analysis and cell multicolor imaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM, HRTEM and FESEM images of CDs (a), Au NPs (b), pSiO2 (c, d), pSiO2-Au (e, f) and pSiO2-Au/HCA (g, h).
N2 adsorption−desorption isotherms (a) and pore size distribution (b) of pSiO2 and pSiO2-Au/HCA.
XPS full spectrum of the CDs (a), high resolution spectrum of C1s (b), N1s(c) and O1s (d).
UV–vis/PL spectra of CDs (a), UV–vis spectra (b) and PL spectra (c) of pSiO2-Au/HCA and CDs-pSiO2-Au/HCA, and FT-IR spectra of CDs (d).
Zeta potentials (a) and TG curves (b) of pSiO2, pSiO2-SH, pSiO2-ss-NH2, pSiO2-Au/HA, pSiO2-Au/HCA.
The release profile of Dox from the pSiO2-Au/HCA carrier in buffer solution with different GSH (a), enzyme (b) and pH (c).
Temperature profile of CDs/Dox-pSiO2-Au/HCA within 10 min of radiation of 650 nm at a power of 200 mW.
The UV–vis absorption spectra (a) and the plots of the change in the absorption maximum (b) of mixture of DBPF and CDs.

Cytotoxicity of CDs, pSiO2-Au/HCA and CDs-pSiO2-Au/HCA to HCT-116 cells without light radiation (a). Cytotoxicity of pSiO2-Au/HCA, CDs-pSiO2-Au/HCA, Dox-pSiO2-Au/HCA and CDs/Dox-pSiO2-Au/HCA to HCT-116 cells with light radiation (650 nm, 200 mW, 10 min) (b). Cytotoxicity of CDs/Dox-pSiO2-Au/HCA to normal human liver cells (7702) without light radiation (c). p values were calculated by Tukey's post-test (***p < 0.001, **p < 0.01, and *p < 0.05). The cell survival viability of Normal human liver cells (7702) treated with CDs/Dox-pSiO2-Au/HCA for 24 h.
CLSM images of Hela cells treated with CDs, Au NPs and CDs-pSiO2-Au/HCA (a). CLSM images of Hela cells treated with Rhodamine 123, Hoechst 33342 and CDs-pSiO2-Au/HCA (b).
Preparation mechanism diagram of drug carrier CDs/Dox-pSiO2-Au/HCA.

head.ce:title: Porous silica nanocarriers with gold/carbon quantum dots for photo-chemotherapy and cellular imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Engineering Research Center for Nanomaterials, Henan University, Kaifeng, 475004, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Engineering Research Center for Nanomaterials, Henan University, Kaifeng, 475004, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The integration of multiple treatment methods for improving therapy efficiency has been widely concerned. Herein, porous silica (pSiO2) carriers with center-radial channels were designed and synthesized, then the photosensitizer carbon dots (CDs) and antitumor drug doxorubicin (Dox) were adsorbed into the channels and Au nanoparticles (NPs) were also loaded in the pores of the pSiO2 carriers. Biocompatible hyaluronic acid/alginate/chitosan (HCA) multilayers were coated onto the pSiO2 carriers to seal the loaded-drug pores. The CDs are capable of generating singlet oxygen and multicolor imaging, and Au NPs have good photothermal effect. The obtained CDs/Dox-pSiO2-Au/HCA not only has enhanced synergistic chemotherapy, photothermal therapy (PTT) and photodynamic therapy (PDT), but also exhibits pH/redox/enzyme-triggered drug release.

Porous silica nanocarriers with center-radial channels were prepared for photo-chemotherapy and cellular imaging.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer is still a worldwide problem that has not yet been overcome, because there are not only many kinds of cancer, but also some mutations. For the time being, surgery, radiotherapy and chemotherapy are the main means of treating cancer. However, both radiotherapy and chemotherapy are not ideal due to the emergence of self-resistance, toxic side effects and the risk of cancer cells spread and transfer [1–3]. Therefore, it is necessary to develop new treatment strategies. One method is to develop a new drug delivery system that with ability to encapsulate anticancer drugs into the interior of the carrier and deliver them to tumor sites [4]. In recent years, various studies have focused on developing nanoparticle-based drug delivery systems (DDSs) for targeting delivery of anticancer drugs. To date, various nanocarriers, such as polymeric micelles [5], hydrogels [6], liposomes [7], cellulose [8], Au nanocages [9] and silica nanoparticles [10–15] have been designed for the administration of anticancer drugs, due to their capability of enhancing drug solubility, improving pharmacokinetics and transporting them to target site. Among them, porous silica (pSiO2) nanocarriers have attracted enormous attention due to their tunable pore size, high specific surface area, good biocompatibility and facile surface functionalization [16]. Especially, the surface functionalization is able to offer long blood circulation time, high loading capacity and controlled drug release. For example, Qiu et al. used surface-modified mesoporous silica to achieve pH/redox-responsive drug delivery system [17].

Other strategy to improve the therapeutic efficacy is called “combination therapy” with the combination of multiple therapies [18]. Photothermal therapy (PTT) and photodynamic therapy (PDT) are very promising cancer treatments using visible or near-infrared light [19–21]. PTT mainly relies on photothermal conversion materials to absorb light and kill tumor cells by localized thermal effect. Au NPs are able to transmit light to thermal energy and their photothermal conversion efficiency could be adjusted by controlling structure and surface modification [22,23], so Au NPs are commonly used to obtain hyperthermia in cancer therapy. For PDT, the light-activated photosensitizers can transfer their excited state energy to triplet oxygen nearby to generate reactive oxygen species (ROS), which can cause cell damage or death [24]. In addition to porphyrins, the carbon quantum dots prepared from chlorophyll have also been proven to be an efficient class of photosensitizer [25].

In the present work, we designed and constructed a porous silica (pSiO2)-based therapy platform which combines pSiO2, Au NPs and CDs (Scheme 1 ): The thiol-functionalized pSiO2 NPs with large pores were prepared by two-step condensation, then linker with disulfide bond and amine group was conjugated on the surface of pSiO2 (pSiO2-ss-NH2) NPs. After doxorubicin (Dox) and CDs were loaded into the pores of pSiO2 carriers, Au NPs were also loaded on the surface of pSiO2 carrier. Upon light irradiation, Au NPs and CDs would be used as PTT and PDT agents, respectively. Finally, hyaluronic acid/chitosan/alginate (HCA) multilayer as gatekeeper was conjugated to encapsulate the loaded drugs. The current pSiO2 system combines phototherapy and chemotherapy in a single system and enables on-demand drug release by redox/pH/enzyme stimuli.

1, 3-dlphenylisobenzofuran (DPBF), 3-mercaptopropyltrimethoxysilane (MPTES, 98%), 2,2′-dithiodipyridine, N-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-N′-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC, 98%), cysteamine, N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, 98%), reduced glutathione (GSH, 98%) and hyaluronidase (Hyal-1) were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China); Dialysis bag MD34 (MWCO: 1000, 8000–14000), doxorubicin hydrochloride (Dox) and hyaluronic acid (HA, 98%) were obtained from Beijing Solarbio Science & Technology Co.; Cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB), n-butanol, sodium hydroxide (NaOH), sodium borohydride (NaBH4) and tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) came from Kermel Chemical Reagent Co. (Tianjin, China). Cyclohexane (C6H12), alginate (ALG), chitosan (CHI, BR), chloroauric acid (HAuCl4·4H2O) and sodium alginate (SA) were provided from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. (Guangdong, China). Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM), fetal bovine serum (FBS), penicillin-streptomycin liquid, 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5- diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) and NH2-PEG-NH2 (molecular weight: 2000) were provided from Shanghai Yare Biotech. Rhodamine 123 (Rh 123) and hoechst 33342 were obtained from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co.

The synthesis of pSiO2-ss-NH2 NPs was based on the reported method with minor modification [26]. 0.5 g of CTAB, 0.3 mg of NaOH, 0.5 mL of n-butanol, 7.5 mL of 0.4 M urea solution and 10 mL of cyclohexane were mixed to form water-surfactant-oil system. With vigorous stirring, 0.5 mL of TEOS was added stepwise into the mixed solution. After stirring for 30 min at room temperature, the mixed solution was heated to 70 °C and held for 20 h. Then, the reaction mixture was cooled, centrifuged and washed to obtain the pSiO2 sample, followed by removing CTAB using hot methanol-HCl solution.

Afterwards, 150 mg of pSiO2 was dispersed in 20 mL of ethanol and then 0.8 mL of MPTES was dropped to the pSiO2 dispersion. After stirring overnight at 80 °C, the thiol functionalized pSiO2 (pSiO2-SH) was isolated by centrifuging and washing (to protect thiol group, the pSiO2-SH sample was treated with GSH solution for 4 h).

Subsequently, 150 mg of pSiO2-SH and 0.75 g of 2,2′-dithiodipyridine were added to 50 mL of absolute ethanol and stirred at 25 °C for 24 h. Afterwards, the acquired product was isolated and dispersed in 30 mL of absolute ethanol, followed by the addition of 20 mL of ethanol solution of cysteine (0.265 g). The reaction was further carried out at room temperature for another 24 h. Sample pSiO2-ss-NH2 was obtained by centrifuging and washing (Scheme 1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDs and Au NPs
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical synthesis of CDs, 50 mg of H2N-PEG-NH2 was dissolved in 20 mL of oleic acid and reacted at 150 °C for 1.5 h under nitrogen protection. Then 5 mL of chlorophyll extract from fresh spinach leaves was added and the reaction was continued at 150 °C for 4 h. After cooling to room temperature, 10 mL of 6 M HCl was added and stirred overnight. Subsequently, the mixture was separated, and the lower liquid was collected and dialyzed in a dialysis bag for 48 h. Finally, the supernatant was centrifuged and freeze-dried to obtain CDs. Au NPs were prepared by the reduction of HAuCl4 using NaBH4 in the presence of sodium citrate according to our previous work [27].

Dox was loaded into pSiO2-ss-NH2 using a simple physical adsorption method. In brief, CDs and Dox were dispersed/dissolved in distilled water with concentrations of 3 mg/mL CDs and 1 mg/mL Dox. Then 12 mg of pSiO2-ss-NH2 carrier was dispersed into 8 mL of above mixed solution. After the suspension was shaken for 24 h at room temperature, the pSiO2-ss-NH2 with CDs and Dox (CDs/Dox-pSiO2-ss-NH2) was obtained by centrifugation and washing. The supernatant and all washing solution were collected to determine the Dox loading by UV absorption.

For encapsulating, 100 mg of CDs/Dox-pSiO2-ss-NH2 was dispersed in solution containing Au NPs and stirred at room temperature for 4 h. At the same time, 100 mg of EDC/NHS was added to 20 mL of HA solution (5 mg/mL) to activate carboxyl group of HA for 1 h. Then, the collected CDs/Dox-pSiO2-ss-Au was dispersed in the activated HA solution and reacted for another 12 h at room temperature to obtain CDs/Dox-pSiO2-ss-Au/HA. Subsequently, the CDs/Dox-pSiO2-ss-Au/HA suspension reacted with 10 mL of 6 mg/mL CHI solution containing 0.5 M NaCl at 25 °C for 2 h. Afterwards, 10 mL of 6 mg/mL ALG solution was added to above suspension and stirred for another 2 h [28]. The resulting CDs/Dox-pSiO2-ss-Au/HA/CHI/ALG (CDs/Dox-pSiO2-ss-Au/HCA) was recovered by centrifuging and washing with 0.5 M NaCl solution.

The morphology of the prepared samples was characterized using transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100 JEOL) and field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, Carl Zeiss, UK). The nitrogen sorption isotherms were measured by using an automated surface area and pore size analyzer (JW-BK222, Jingwei Gaobo) at 77 K. The surface area and pore size were calculated from the adsorption branches of isotherms by Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) method. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed using thermogravimetric analyzer (METTLER TOLEDO, Switzerland) under air flow. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were acquired with a AVATAR360 spectrometer using KBr pellet. X -ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo ESCALAB 250XI) was applied to identify the chemical composition. The optical property was measured on UV–vis absorption spectrometer (CARY60, Malaysia) and fluorescence spectrometer (Cary Eclipse, Malaysia). Zeta potentials were conducted on the Zetasizer (Malvern, Nano ZS) at 25 °C. Laser source was from semiconductor CW laser (KYD650NX-T1685, Shenzhen). Cell imaging and localization analysis were performed using structured illumination ultra-high resolution microscopic imaging system (Japan, Nikon/A1+N-SIM).

The drug release from CDs/Dox-pSiO2-Au/HCA was performed in following release media: (1) phosphate buffer saline (PBS) solution with different pH values (4.0, 5.0, 6.0 and 7.4); (2) PBS solution at pH 7.4 with varying GSH concentrations (0.1, 1.0 and 10.0 mg/mL); (3) PBS solution at pH 7.4 with different Hyal-1 concentrations (0.1, 0.25 and 0.5 mM); (4) PBS solution at pH 4.0 containing 10.0 mg/mL GSH and 0.5 mM Hyal-1.

For drug release, 5 mg of CDs/Dox-pSiO2-Au/HCA was dispersed into 5 mL of distilled water and then dispersion was transferred into a dialysis bag. Subsequently, the bag was immersed in 30 mL of above release media at 37 °C for 36 h in the dark. At different times, 1 mL of PBS solution was taken out to calculate the released Dox concentration by measuring the UV absorbance at 481 nm and 1 mL of fresh medium was added to keep the volume constant.

To study the photothermal effect, CDs/Dox-pSiO2-Au/HCA was dispersed in water to form suspension with different concentrations (100, 200, 500 μg/mL). Then 1 mL of suspension was transferred to a 10 mm colorimetric dish, and irradiated with a 650 nm red laser at 200 mW for 10 min. To explore the effect of irradiated power, 1 mL of 200 μg/mL suspension was exposed to laser at different powers (0.2, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0 W). The temperature variation of the suspensions was recorded at a fixed time point using a thermocouple digital thermometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cytotoxicity against HCT-116 cells
ce:section.ce:para: MTT assay was used to assess the cytotoxicity of the samples against HCT-116 cells [29]. The HCT-116 cells are seeded in 96-well plates with a density of 6 × 103/well and cultured in DMEM medium (including 10% FBS, 0.1% penicillin-streptomycin, and 5% CO2) at 37 °C for 24 h. After that, the previous medium was discarded and fresh medium containing CDs, pSiO2-Au/HCA and CDs-pSiO2-Au/HCA at different concentrations (0, 50, 100, 200 and 400 μg/mL) was added. After cells was cultured at 37 °C and 5% CO2 for 24 h, the culture medium containing sample was aspirated, and 10 μL of 5 mg/mL of MTT solution was supplemented. The cells were incubated for an additional 4 h, the cell viability was obtained by MTT. For photodynamic cytotoxicity, the cells were exposed to the solutions of pSiO2-Au/HCA, CDs-pSiO2-Au/HCA, Dox-pSiO2-Au/HCA and CDs/Dox-pSiO2-Au/HCA at concentration of 0, 5, 10, 20, and 40 μg/mL, and the culture medium was irradiated at 200 mW with a 650 nm laser for 10 min before incubation. The cell incubation and measurement of cell viability were carried out at the same conditions as described above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell multicolor imaging and intracellular localization
ce:section.ce:para: Hela cells in logarithmic growth phase was placed on laser confocal special dishes with 1 × 105/well and cultured in DMEM medium at 37 °C for 24 h. The old culture medium was then replaced by 1 mL of 100 μg/mL culture solution of CDs, Au NPs and CDs-pSiO2-Au/HCA. After culturing for another 12 h, the culture solution was discard and the cells were washed with PBS thrice. The cell image was captured with confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) at excitation wavelengths of 405 nm and 488 nm. The cells were also stained with Rhodamine 123 and Hoechst 33342 for localization analysis.

Fig. 1 a depicts the typical TEM and HRTEM images of the CDs sample. The CDs have average particle diameter of 2.0 nm. The lattice fringe spacing is about 0.22 nm, which is close to the (100) crystal plane spacing of graphene [30]. Similar to CDs, the synthesized Au NPs have an average diameter of 5.6 nm (Fig. 1b). Fig. 1c-h give the TEM and FESEM images of the prepared pSiO2, pSiO2-Au and pSiO2-Au/HCA samples. The pSiO2 sample presents spherical shape with size of 280 nm and has divergent pore structure (Fig. 1c and d). Due to the presence of thiol groups, Au NPs can be easy bond on the surface of pSiO2 sample. After binding Au NPs, there are a large number of dark spots on the surface of pSiO2 sphere (Fig. 1e and f). After coating HCA, the pores of the pSiO2 sample become blurred, indicating the presence of HCA layer (Fig. 1g and h).

The porous feature of pSiO2 and pSiO2-Au/HCA was characterized by N2 sorption measurement. Fig. 2 depicts the N2 sorption isotherms and corresponding pore size distribution curves of both samples. It can be seen that both samples show a typical IV isotherm with a distinct hysteresis loop, indicating the existence of mesopores [31]. The specific surface area and total pore volume of pSiO2 sample are 371 m2/g and 0.82 cm3/g, respectively. After HCA modification, specific surface area and total pore volume decrease to 242 m2/g and 0.76 cm3/g, which indicates the successful modification. Both pore size distribution curves have a peak position centered at 4.4 nm.

The chemical composition of the CDs sample was analyzed by XPS. The XPS spectrum of CDs shows three peaks at 284.8 eV, 399.86 eV and 532 eV, which are assigned to C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively (Fig. 3 a). The C1S spectrum can be curve-fitted into four peaks at 284.2, 285.1, 284.6, and 286.2 eV, which are attributed to CN/CC, C–O, C–N, and CO, respectively (Fig. 3b). The binding energies of N1s are found at 400.2 eV and 399.6 eV, corresponding to pyrrole-like N and pyridine-like N atoms, respectively (Fig. 3c) [30,32]. Furthermore, the high resolution of O1s spectrum (Fig. 3d) reveals the presence of O–N (532.4 eV), OC (531.7eV) and O–C (532.9 eV) bond [30,33].

Fig. 4 a shows the adsorption and PL spectra of the prepared CDs. At excitation of 420 nm, CDs sample generates two emission peaks at 506 and 676 nm, and the latter is close to the peak of the porphyrin derivative [30]. In addition, the adsorption and emission spectra of pSiO2-Au/HCA and CDs-pSiO2-Au/HCA are also given (Fig. 4b and c). Compared with CDs and Au NPs, the adsorption and PL intensity of both samples were weakened a lot, which may be related to electronic interactions between Au NPs (CDs) and HA molecules. Fig. 4d presents the FTIR spectra of the prepared CDs. In the FTIR spectrum, the typical absorption peak at 3431 cm−1 corresponds to –NH2/-OH vibration. Three characteristic peaks at 2925, 2864 and 1107 cm−1 agree well with the methyl, methylene and C–O stretching vibration. The peaks at 1643, 1460 and 1726 cm−1 are assigned to CC/CN stretching vibration, N–H bending vibration and CO stretching vibration, respectively [33].

Fig. 5 a gives the zeta potentials of the prepared samples. The potential of bare silica is about −25.6 mV due to the presence of silanol groups. After thiol-functionalization, the potential increases to −22.9 mV [34]. After conjugation with cysteamine, the potential is further increased to −11.3 mV due to the presence of positively charged amino groups. When HCA is coating, the potential decreases from −11.3 mV to −40.7 mV. The corresponding changes of zeta potentials provide evidence for each step modification. Fig. 5b gives the TG curves of the prepared samples. It is clear that all samples display a successive mass loss process from room temperature to 1000 °C. The pSiO2 sample presents negligible weight loss (only 5%), while the modified samples show much weight loss (pSiO2-SH 11%, pSiO2-ss-NH2 13%, pSiO2-Au/HA 21%, pSiO2-Au/HCA 42%). Generally, the release of adsorbed water takes place below 100 °C and the subsequent mass loss is attributed to the decomposition of organic moiety and Si–OH dehydration [35]. The TG difference from the decomposition of organic moiety confirmed each step modification.

The dendritic pores of carriers are suitable to transport and load drug molecules and the drug-loading capacity for Dox is 18.7%. The responsive release of the CDs/Dox-pSiO2-Au/HCA was carried out in different simulated media (Fig. 6 ). Due to the presence of disulfide bond and hyaluronic acid in “gatekeeper” layer, the release of Dox would be triggered by pH, GSH and enzyme. Clearly, only a small amount of Dox released from CDs/Dox-pSiO2-Au/HCA in PBS (pH 7.4, without stimuli species), indicating that the HCA layer can effectively encapsulate the mesoporous channel and avoid drug leakage. The presence of GSH species could cleave the disulfide bond in the linker molecules and hyaluronidase (Hyal-1) could degrade HA, which would induce the detachment of HCA layer, promoting the drug release. As expected, Dox release from CDs/Dox-pSiO2-Au/HCA increased dramatically once GSH was added to the system (Fig. 6a). In the presence of 0.1 mM GSH, the cumulative release of Dox is about 16.1% within 36 h. At 10.0 mM GSH, the cumulative Dox release is 34.3%. Similarly, the release of Dox is also increased in the presence of Hyal-1(Fig. 6b). It is well known that pH in tumor tissues is lower than that in normal blood due to the over rapid metabolism. Fig. 6c illustrates the cumulative release of Dox under different pH values. Clearly, the accumulative release of Dox increases with the decrease of pH value. The release of Dox at pH 4.0 within 36 h is about 5 times of that at pH 7.4. Furthermore, the coexistence of GSH and hyaluronidase gave a faster release of Dox from the CDs/Dox-pSiO2-Au/HCA with higher efficiency due to the simultaneous breaking of the disulfide bond and HA. For instance, the release of Dox at pH 4.0 PBS with 10.0 mM GSH and 0.5 mg/mL Hyal-1 was up to 75%, which was 7 times of that at pH 7.4 without GSH/Hyal-1 within 36 h. In acidic environment, HCA layer expands gradually to form porous structure or local defect, which is beneficial to the penetration of drug molecules [36]. In addition, with the decrease of pH value, the increase of electrostatic repulsion between molecules as well as between molecules and carriers may be another important factor for accelerating drug release.

Fig. 7 a depicts temperature change of CDs/Dox-pSiO2-Au/HCA suspensions with different concentrations (100, 200, 500 μg/mL) under irradiation of 650 nm for 10 min. Clearly, the change of temperature in pure water is almost negligible, while the temperature of sample suspensions gradually increases with irradiation time going. For the suspension at 200 μg/mL, the temperature increased from 30 to 43 °C within 10 min, indicating a rapid conversion of the NIR energy into thermal energy. As the concentration further increased, the temperature of the suspensions is markedly improved. Compared with pure water, the temperature of three suspensions irradiated for 10 min is increased by 5.3 °C, 10.4 °C and 17.6 °C, respectively. The excellent photothermal performance is ascribed to Au NPs on CDs/Dox-pSiO2-Au/HCA.

To study the effect of power density on temperature change, 200 μg/mL CDs/Dox-pSiO2-Au/HCA suspension was exposed to the laser with different power densities for 10 min. Fig. 7b shows the temperature variation of the sample suspension with irradiation power. As expected, the temperature change displays a distinct laser power dependence, and the temperature is increased to 42.6 and 64.1 °C at 0.2 and 2.0 W/cm2, respectively. Generally, 42 °C is sufficient to induce apoptosis and the tumor can be successfully removed at 42 °C for 15–60 min [37], so. the temperature elevation at the power density of 0.2 W/cm2 is enough for PTT.

Singlet oxygen (1O2) can induce apoptosis and even lead to necrosis through oxidative stress [38]. The ability of CDs to generate 1O2 was determined by DBPF method. Fig. 8 a gives the absorption of the mixture of CDs with DPBF under laser irradiation. Clearly, the absorbance of DPBF at 410 nm decreases continuously with irradiation time going. Within 3 min, the absorbance was reduced by 80%, indicating the generation of singlet oxygen. Fig. 8b displays the variation of absorption of DPBF with irradiation time. Without CDs or with CDs under dark condition, the absorption intensity of DPBF remains unchanged under laser irradiation, indicating no singlet oxygen generation. In the presence of light and CDs, the absorbance is significantly reduced, which confirms that the CDs could be activated by absorbing light and generate singlet oxygen.

Drug carriers should have low cytotoxicity and good biocompatibility and the MTT assay was conducted to determine the cytotoxicity of the carrier and Dox-loaded carrier against HCT-116 cells. Fig. 9 a shows the cell viability of HCT-116 cells after being incubated with CDs, pSiO2-Au/HCA and CDs-pSiO2-Au/HCA without light radiation. It can be seen that there is no significant difference in relative cell viability for three samples, and they are not significant cytotoxic in the concentration range of 0–400 μg/mL. When the sample concentration was up to 400 mg/mL, the cell survival rate was still more than 87%, indicating that the synthesized samples have good biocompatibility. In the presence of light, pSiO2-Au/HCA, CDs-pSiO2-Au/HCA display significant cytotoxicity and the cell viability is markedly decreased with increasing concentration (Fig. 9b). When the concentration was 40 mg/mL, the cell survival rates were 56.33% for pSiO2-Au/HCA and 61.64% for CDs-pSiO2-Au/HCA, respectively, indicating the presence of photo-thermal/dynamic effect. After loading Dox, the lower cell viability occurs for the drug-loaded carrier in comparison with the blank carrier. The cell viability is about 38.7% for CDs/Dox-pSiO2-Au/HCA, which is lower than that for Dox-pSiO2-Au/HCA (50.8%). The higher cytotoxicity was related to the synergistic chemo-photothermal therapy and chemo-photothermal/dynamic therapy. For normal human liver cells (7702), the cell viability is related to the concentration of the CDs/Dox-pSiO2-Au/HCA sample (Fig. 9c).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Location analysis and cell multicolor imaging
ce:section.ce:para: The prepared CDs can be excited by blue light, and emit green light and red light, which would be used for multicolor cell imaging. Based on the double emission characteristics, the CDs can be used to study the uptake ability of cells to CDs or pSiO2 carriers. Fig. 10 a shows the CLSM images of Hela cells treated with CDs, Au NPs and CDs-pSiO2-Au/HCA for 12 h. Under the excitation of 405 nm, the HeLa cells treated with CDs exhibited significant green and red fluorescence. The cell treated with Au NPs have negligible fluorescent signal. As expected, the cells treated with CDs-pSiO2-Au/HCA also displayed visible fluorescence in both channels (green and red), with uneven fluorescence intensity. The CLSM images showed that the CDs and CDs-pSiO2-Au/HCA were effectively internalized and CDs can be used for multicolor imaging of cells. To study the distribution of CDs-pSiO2-Au/HCA, HeLa cells were treated with Hoechst 33342, Rh 123 and CDs-pSiO2-Au/HCA. Fig. 10b displays the CLSM images of the treated Hela cells. Blue fluorescence is attributed to the position of the nucleus, green fluorescence is composed of rhodamine 123 and carrier fluorescence, and red fluorescence is derived from the carrier. It can be seen from the merged image that the red fluorescence and blue fluorescence hardly overlap, indicating that the carrier is mainly distributed in the cytoplasm.

In summary, we have constructed a multifunctional drug delivery platform combining CDs, Au NPs and porous silica carriers for combination therapy. In this system, CDs and Dox were loaded in the channels of the carriers, Au NPs are conjugated to the outer surface of pSiO2 carrier, and HCA layer is used to encapsulate the drug molecules. The CDs/Dox-pSiO2-Au/HCA display photosensitive property and remarkable photothermal conversion capacity. The release of Dox could be controlled by reducing, acidic and enzyme responses. The cytotoxicity assays also demonstrated that obtained carriers have good biocompatibility and low toxicity.
All authors have seen and agreed with the contents of the revised manuscript and order of authors.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts of interest to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by Medical Interdisciplinary training Program of Henan University in 2019 (CJ1205A0240013), Interdisciplinary Research for First-Class Discipline Construction Project of Henan University (grant No. 2019YLXKJC04) and Graduate Education Innovation and Quality Improvement Program of Henan University (SYL19060105).
